docs.title: gitmap tag
docs.text: Create or list version tags.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: gitmap tag [OPTIONS] [NAME]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --message / -m Annotate the tag with a message --list List all tags

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Create a lightweight tag
gitmap tag v1.0.0

# Create an annotated tag
gitmap tag v1.0.0 -m "First production release"

# List all tags
gitmap tag --list

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: Git-Map adapts Git's mental model to web maps. If you know Git, you already know most of this.

docs.title: Repository
docs.text:
A repository (.gitmap/ directory) is the version-control database for a project. It stores every commit, branch pointer, and config value. One repository typically tracks one logical map project.

my-maps/
└── .gitmap/
    ├── config.json      # Project settings
    ├── HEAD             # Points to current branch
    ├── index.json       # Staged map data (working area)
    ├── refs/heads/      # Branch pointers
    └── objects/commits/ # Commit snapshots

docs.title: Commit
docs.text:
A commit is a snapshot of the map's JSON at a point in time. Each commit stores:

  • The full operationalLayers and map settings
  • Author, timestamp, and message
  • A reference to the parent commit (forming a chain)

Commits are immutable. You can always return to any commit with gitmap revert or gitmap checkout.

docs.title: Branch
docs.text:
A branch is just a named pointer to a commit. Branches let you maintain parallel versions of a map — for example:

  • main → production map
  • feature/new-symbology → experimental changes
  • staging → QA-ready version

Branches are cheap and fast. Create them freely.

docs.title: Index (Staging Area)
docs.text: The index (index.json) holds the current map data pulled from Portal or manually staged. Running gitmap commit snapshots the index into a new commit.

docs.title: Remote
docs.text: The remote is an ArcGIS Online or Portal organization. gitmap push writes the current branch to Portal as a web map item; gitmap pull reads it back.

docs.title: HEAD
docs.text:
HEAD points to the current branch (or a specific commit in detached-HEAD mode). Most commands operate on HEAD.

Next: CLI Reference →

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.11, 3.12, or 3.13
  • ArcGIS Online account or Portal for ArcGIS 10.8+

docs.title: Install from PyPI
docs.text:
pip install gitmap-core

Verify the install:

gitmap --version

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/14-TR/Git-Map.git
cd Git-Map
pip install -e "packages/gitmap_core"

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Git-Map reads credentials from environment variables when no config is set:

Variable Description ARCGIS_USERNAME Your ArcGIS/Portal username ARCGIS_PASSWORD Your ArcGIS/Portal password PORTAL_URL Your Portal URL (defaults to https://www.arcgis.com)

You can also store credentials in the repository config file — see Working with Portals.

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text:
pip install --upgrade gitmap-core

Next: Quickstart →

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Get from zero to your first committed map version in under 5 minutes.

docs.title: 1. Initialize a Repository
docs.text:
Navigate to a working directory and initialize Git-Map:

mkdir my-maps
cd my-maps
gitmap init --project-name "My Maps" --user-name "Your Name"

This creates a .gitmap/ directory that tracks all your map versions.

docs.title: 2. Pull a Map from Portal
docs.text:
Authenticate and pull a web map from ArcGIS Online or Portal:

gitmap pull --url https://www.arcgis.com --branch main

Git-Map fetches the map JSON and stages it locally. Nothing is committed yet.

docs.title: 3. Check Status
docs.text:
gitmap status

You'll see something like:

╭─ GitMap Status ─╮
│ On branch: main │
╰─────────────────╯
Changes not committed:
  Staged map with 4 layer(s)
Use "gitmap commit -m <message>" to commit changes

docs.title: 4. Commit
docs.text:
gitmap commit -m "Initial snapshot of production map"

Output:

Created commit a3f2c1b0

  Message:   Initial snapshot of production map
  Author:    Your Name
  Layers:    4

Branch 'main' updated to a3f2c1b0

docs.title: 5. Create a Branch and Experiment
docs.text:
gitmap branch feature/new-basemap
gitmap checkout feature/new-basemap

Make your changes (edit the map in Portal), then pull and commit again:

gitmap pull
gitmap commit -m "Switched to dark basemap"

docs.title: 6. View History
docs.text: gitmap log

docs.title: 7. Diff Two Versions
docs.text: gitmap diff

docs.title: 8. Merge and Push
docs.text:
When the feature is ready, merge it back and push to Portal:

gitmap checkout main
gitmap merge feature/new-basemap
gitmap push

Next: Core Concepts →

docs.title: Branching Strategy
docs.text: Git-Map branches are lightweight — create as many as you need. This guide suggests a strategy that works well for most teams.

docs.title: Recommended Branch Layout
docs.text:
main          ← production (always deployable)
staging       ← QA / client review
feature/*     ← individual changes
hotfix/*      ← urgent production fixes

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
feature/x  →  staging  →  main
hotfix/y                →  main
  1. Create a feature/ branch for each change.
  2. Merge to staging for review/QA.
  3. Merge staging to main and push to production.
  4. For urgent fixes, branch from main as hotfix/, fix, merge directly to main.

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Prefix Use Case feature/ New layers, symbology changes, data updates hotfix/ Urgent production fixes experiment/ Risky changes you might not keep archive/ Preserved state of a historical map version

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Keep branches short-lived — merge and delete when done.
  • Use gitmap stash to quickly save work-in-progress before switching branches.
  • Tag stable states before major changes: gitmap tag pre-overhaul.

docs.title: Working with Portals
docs.text: Git-Map supports both ArcGIS Online and Portal for ArcGIS (10.8+).

docs.title: Environment Variables (recommended for CI/CD)
docs.text:
export ARCGIS_USERNAME=jsmith
export ARCGIS_PASSWORD=mypassword
export PORTAL_URL=https://myorg.maps.arcgis.com

docs.title: Per-Repo Config
docs.text:
Store the remote URL in the repo config so you don't have to pass --url every time:

gitmap config --remote-url https://myorg.maps.arcgis.com
gitmap config --username jsmith

This writes to .gitmap/config.json. Do not commit credentials to source control.

docs.title: ArcGIS Online
docs.text:
The default Portal URL is https://www.arcgis.com. Named-user authentication is used.

gitmap pull --url https://www.arcgis.com

docs.title: Portal for ArcGIS
docs.text:
Replace with your organization's Portal URL:

gitmap pull --url https://portal.myorg.com/portal

docs.title: Item Organization
docs.text:
When you push, Git-Map creates a GitMap folder in your Portal content and names items using the pattern:

{project-name} [{branch}]

This keeps branches organized and easy to find.

docs.title: Notifications
docs.text:
When pushing to the configured production branch, Git-Map can send Portal notifications to all users in groups that have access to the map. Use --no-notify to suppress this.

gitmap push              # notifies group members
gitmap push --no-notify  # silent push

docs.title: Day-to-Day Workflow
docs.text: This guide walks through the standard Git-Map workflow for managing a production web map.

docs.title: The Golden Rule
docs.text: Never edit the production map directly. Always work on a branch, review the diff, and merge when ready.

docs.title: 1. Start on main, sync first
docs.text:
cd my-map-project
gitmap checkout main
gitmap pull
gitmap commit -m "Sync: latest production state"

docs.title: 2. Create a feature branch
docs.text:
gitmap branch feature/updated-symbology
gitmap checkout feature/updated-symbology

docs.title: 3. Make changes in Portal
docs.text:
Edit the map in ArcGIS Online or Portal as normal. When done:

gitmap pull --branch feature/updated-symbology
gitmap diff           # Review what changed
gitmap commit -m "Updated parcel symbology per client request"

docs.title: 4. Merge and deploy
docs.text:
gitmap checkout main
gitmap merge feature/updated-symbology
gitmap push

docs.title: 5. Tag releases (optional)
docs.text: gitmap tag v2.3 -m "Q2 2024 symbology update"

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
If something goes wrong after a push:

gitmap log --oneline        # Find the last good commit
gitmap revert a3f2c1b0      # Restore that state
gitmap push                 # Deploy the fix
